questions.question: If log7 5 = a, log5 3 = b and log3 2 = c, then the logarithm of the number 70 to the base 225 is

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 14 November 2021
questions.question: If log10 2 log2 10 + log10(10x) = 2, then what is the value of x?
options: log52
questions.solution:
Let's break down the equation step by step:

The given equation is:

$$\log_{10} 2 \cdot \log_2 10 + \log_{10}(10^x) = 2.$$

Notice that the logarithm of an exponent simplifies as follows:

$$\log_{10}(10^x) = x.$$

So, the equation becomes:

$$\log_{10} 2 \cdot \log_2 10 + x = 2.$$

A key logarithmic identity is:

$$\log_a b \cdot \log_b a = 1.$$

Here, with $ a = 10 $ and $ b = 2 $, we have:

$$\log_{10} 2 \cdot \log_2 10 = 1.$$

Substitute this value back into the equation:

$$1 + x = 2.$$

Solve for $ x $:

$$x = 2 - 1 = 1.$$

Thus, the value of $ x $ is 1, which corresponds to Option B.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 18 April 2021
questions.question: The value of x, satisfying the equation  $log_{cos x} ~sin x = 1$, where $0<x<\dfrac{\pi}{2}$, is
questions.solution:
The given equation is

$$ \log_{\cos x}(\sin x) = 1. $$

This implies:

$$ \sin x = (\cos x)^1 = \cos x. $$

Thus, we have:

$$ \sin x = \cos x. $$

By dividing both sides by $\cos x$, we get:

$$ \tan x = 1. $$

The solutions to $\tan x = 1$ are given by:

$$ x = \frac{\pi}{4} + k\pi $$

where $k \in \mathbb{Z}$. However, the constraint is $0 < x < \frac{\pi}{2}$. Therefore, the solution that satisfies this condition is:

$$ x = \frac{\pi}{4}. $$

Therefore, the value of $x$ satisfying the equation is:

Option C: $$\frac{\pi}{4}$$.

questions.title: NDA Mathematics 18 April 2021
questions.question:
If n = 100!, then what is the value of the following?

$\rm \dfrac{1}{log_2n}+\dfrac{1}{log_3n}+\dfrac{1}{log_4n}+{.....}+\dfrac{1}{log_{100}n}$
questions.solution:
Concept

Product Rule: The logarithm of a product is equal to the sum of the logarithms of the individual factors.

$ \log_a(mn) = \log_a m + \log_a n $

Change of Base Rule: This relates logarithms of different bases to each other.

$ \log_m n = \frac{1}{\log_n m} $

Calculation

Given the expression:

$ \frac{1}{\log_2 n} + \frac{1}{\log_3 n} + \frac{1}{\log_4 n} + \ldots + \frac{1}{\log_{100} n} $

Using the change of base rule, this can be simplified to:

$ \log_n 2 + \log_n 3 + \log_n 4 + \ldots + \log_n 100 $

This sum is equivalent to:

$ \log_n (2 \times 3 \times 4 \times \ldots \times 100) $

Which simplifies to:

$ \log_n (100!) $

Since $ n = 100! $, the expression becomes:

$ \log_{100!} (100!) $

This evaluates to:

$ 1 $

Thus, the value of the given expression is 1.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics (Term 1) Paper 2021-22
questions.question: The horizontal component of earth's magnetic field at a place is 0.2 G whereas it's total magnetic field is 0.4 G. The angle of dip at the place is
questions.solution:
To determine the angle of dip (also known as the magnetic inclination) at a place, we can use the relationship between the horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field (BH), the total magnetic field (B), and the angle of dip (δ). The formula connecting these quantities is given by:

$$B_H = B \cos \delta$$

Where:

  • $$B_H$$ is the horizontal component of the Earth's magnetic field
  • $$B$$ is the total magnetic field
  • $$\delta$$ is the angle of dip

Given that:

$$B_H = 0.2 \, \text{G}$$

$$B = 0.4 \, \text{G}$$

We need to find the angle δ. Rearranging the formula to solve for δ gives:

$$\cos \delta = \frac{B_H}{B}$$

Substitute the given values:

$$\cos \delta = \frac{0.2}{0.4} = 0.5$$

The angle whose cosine is 0.5 is 60°:

$$\delta = \cos^{-1}(0.5) = 60^\circ$$

Therefore, the angle of dip at the place is 60°.

So, the correct answer is:

Option C

60$$^\circ$$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics (Term 1) Paper 2021-22
questions.question: Which of the following statements is correct?
options: Magnetic field lines do not form closed loops.
Magnetic field lines start from north pole and end at south pole of a magnet.
The tangent at a point on a magnetic field line represents the direction of the magnetic field at that point.
Two magnetic field lines may intersect each other.
questions.solution:
Let's analyze the given statements to determine which one is correct.

Option A: Magnetic field lines do not form closed loops.

This statement is incorrect. Magnetic field lines do indeed form closed loops. They do not start or end anywhere in space but rather loop back from the north pole to the south pole outside the magnet and continue from the south pole to the north pole inside the magnet. Hence, they are continuous and form closed loops.

Option B: Magnetic field lines start from north pole and end at south pole of a magnet.

This statement is also incorrect in the strictest sense. While it is true that outside a magnet, magnetic field lines appear to originate from the magnet's north pole and terminate at the south pole, this does not account for the entire loop. Inside the magnet, the field lines continue from the south pole back to the north pole, forming a closed loop.

Option C: The tangent at a point on a magnetic field line represents the direction of the magnetic field at that point.

This statement is correct. The direction of the magnetic field at any point in space is given by the direction of the tangent to the field line at that point. More formally, if you draw a tangent to the field line at any point, the tangent's direction is the same as the direction of the magnetic field at that point.

Option D: Two magnetic field lines may intersect each other.

This statement is incorrect. Two magnetic field lines can never intersect each other. If they did, it would imply that there are two different directions of the magnetic field at the point of intersection, which is not possible.

Thus, the correct statement is:

Option C: The tangent at a point on a magnetic field line represents the direction of the magnetic field at that point.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics (Term 1) Paper 2021-22
questions.question: A bar magnet has magnetic dipole moment $$\overrightarrow M $$. Its initial position is parallel to the direction of uniform magnetic field $$\overrightarrow B $$. In this position, the magnitudes of torque and force acting on it respectively are -
questions.solution:
To analyze the situation, let's break down the concepts of torque and force acting on a bar magnet in a uniform magnetic field.

The torque $$\overrightarrow \tau $$ on a magnetic dipole with magnetic moment $$\overrightarrow M $$ in a uniform magnetic field $$\overrightarrow B $$ is given by the cross product:

$$\overrightarrow \tau = \overrightarrow M \times \overrightarrow B $$

The magnitude of this torque is:

$$\tau = |\overrightarrow M| |\overrightarrow B| \sin \theta$$

where $$\theta$$ is the angle between $$\overrightarrow M $$ and $$\overrightarrow B$$. In this problem, the bar magnet is initially positioned parallel to the direction of the uniform magnetic field. Hence, $$\theta = 0$$ degrees. As a result, the magnitude of the torque is:

$$\tau = M B \sin(0) = 0$$

Therefore, the torque acting on the bar magnet is zero.

Next, let's consider the force acting on the bar magnet. In a uniform magnetic field, the magnetic force on a magnetic dipole is zero, because a uniform magnetic field exerts equal and opposite forces on the north and south poles of the magnet, resulting in a net force of zero. Mathematically, the net force $$\overrightarrow F $$ on a magnetic dipole in a uniform magnetic field is given as:

$$\overrightarrow F = 0$$

As a result, the force acting on the bar magnet is also zero.

Based on the above analysis, the correct option is:

Option C

0 and 0

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics (Term 1) Paper 2021-22
questions.question:
Assertion (A) : The poles of a bar magnet cannot be separated.

Reason (R) : Magnetic monopoles do not exist.
options: Both (A) & (R) are true and (R) is correct explanation of (A).
Both (A) & (R) are true and (R) is not correct explanation of (A).
(A) is false and (R) is also false.
questions.solution:
Let's analyze both the assertion (A) and the reason (R) provided in the question:

Assertion (A): The poles of a bar magnet cannot be separated.

Reason (R): Magnetic monopoles do not exist.

First, let’s understand the assertion. A bar magnet has two poles: the North pole and the South pole. If a bar magnet is cut into two pieces, each piece will still have both a North and South pole. This is because the magnetic field lines are continuous loops extending from the North pole to the South pole. Therefore, the assertion that the poles of a bar magnet cannot be separated is true.

Next, let’s understand the reason. A magnetic monopole would be a hypothetical particle or object that has only one magnetic pole (either North or South). However, in all experiments and observations so far, isolated magnetic monopoles have never been detected. This means that every magnetic source always has a dipole, which implies that magnetic monopoles do not exist. Therefore, the reason provided is also true.

Now, we need to determine if the reason correctly explains the assertion. The statement "The poles of a bar magnet cannot be separated" is a direct consequence of the non-existence of magnetic monopoles. If magnetic monopoles did exist, it would be possible to isolate the North and South poles from each other. Since they don't exist, cutting a magnet always results in two dipoles. Therefore, the reason correctly explains the assertion.

Given this, the correct option is:

A) Both (A) & (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics (Term 1) Paper 2021-22
questions.question: Two wires carrying currents I1 and I2 lie, one slightly above the other, in a horizontal plane as shown in figure. The region of vertically upward strongest magnetic field is
questions.solution: Applying right hand thumb rule, the strongest magnetic field will be in region II.

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics (Term 1) Paper 2021-22
questions.question: Two parallel conductors carrying current of 4.0 A and 10.0 A are placed 2.5 cm apart in vacuum. The force per unit length between them is -
questions.solution:
To find the force per unit length between two parallel conductors carrying currents, we use Ampère's force law. The formula for the force per unit length between two parallel wires separated by a distance $ d $ and carrying currents $ I_1 $ and $ I_2 $ is given by:

$$F/L = \frac{{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}}{{2\pi d}}$$

where:

  • $$F/L$$ is the force per unit length between the conductors
  • $$\mu_0$$ is the permeability of free space, which is $$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{T} \cdot \text{m/A}$$ (tesla meter per ampere)
  • $$I_1$$ and $$I_2$$ are the currents carried by the conductors
  • $$d$$ is the distance between the conductors

Given values:

  • $$I_1 = 4.0 \, \text{A}$$
  • $$I_2 = 10.0 \, \text{A}$$
  • $$d = 2.5 \, \text{cm} = 0.025 \, \text{m}$$

Now substituting these values into the formula:

$$F/L = \frac{{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(4.0)(10.0)}}{{2\pi (0.025)}}$$

Let's simplify the expression:

$$F/L = \frac{{4 \times 10^{-7} \times 4.0 \times 10.0}}{{2 \times 0.025}}$$

$$F/L = \frac{{16 \times 10^{-7} \times 10}}{{0.05}}$$

$$F/L = \frac{{160 \times 10^{-7}}}{{0.05}}$$

$$F/L = 32 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{N/m}$$

Thus,

$$F/L = 3.2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{N/m}$$

Therefore, the closest option is:

Option D: 3.2 $$\times$$ 10$$-$$4 N/m

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics (Term 1) Paper 2021-22
questions.question: The magnetic field at the centre of a current carrying circular loop of radius R is B<sub>1</sub>. The magnetic field at a point on its axis at a distance R from the centre of the loop is B<sub>2</sub>. Then the ratio (B<sub>1</sub> / B<sub>2</sub>) is
questions.solution:
To find the ratio of the magnetic field at the center of a current-carrying circular loop ($$B_1$$) to the magnetic field on its axis at a distance $$R$$ from the center of the loop ($$B_2$$), we need to use the formulas for these fields.

The magnetic field at the center of the loop is given by:

$$B_1 = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R}$$

where $$\mu_0$$ is the permeability of free space, $$I$$ is the current, and $$R$$ is the radius of the loop.

The magnetic field at a point on the axis of the loop at a distance $$R$$ from the center is given by:

$$B_2 = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2 (R^2 + R^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2 (2R^2)^{3/2}} = \frac{\mu_0 I R^2}{2 \cdot 2\sqrt{2} R^3} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \sqrt{2} R}$$

Now, we can find the ratio $$\frac{B_1}{B_2}$$:

$$\frac{B_1}{B_2} = \frac{\frac{\mu_0 I}{2R}}{\frac{\mu_0 I}{4 \sqrt{2} R}} = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2R} \cdot \frac{4 \sqrt{2} R}{\mu_0 I} = \frac{4 \sqrt{2} R}{2R} = 2 \sqrt{2}$$

So, the ratio $$\frac{B_1}{B_2}$$ is $$2 \sqrt{2}$$.

The correct option is:

Option A $$2 \sqrt 2$$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics (Term 1) Paper 2021-22
questions.question: A current carrying wire kept in a uniform magnetic field, will experience a maximum force when it is
options: at an angle of 45$$^\circ$$ to the magnetic field
at an angle of 60$$^\circ$$ to the magnetic field
questions.solution:
In physics, when a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field, it experiences a force. This force is given by the Lorentz force law, which states that the force $$\vec{F}$$ on a current-carrying wire in a magnetic field is given by:

$$ \vec{F} = I (\vec{L} \times \vec{B}) $$

Here, $$I$$ is the current, $$\vec{L}$$ is the length vector of the wire, and $$\vec{B}$$ is the magnetic field. The magnitude of this force is given by:

$$ F = I L B \sin \theta $$

where:

  • $$F$$ is the magnitude of the force
  • $$I$$ is the current flowing through the wire
  • $$L$$ is the length of the wire in the field
  • $$B$$ is the magnetic field strength
  • $$\theta$$ is the angle between the direction of the current and the magnetic field

To experience a maximum force, the value of $$\sin \theta$$ must be at its maximum, which is 1. This happens when $$\theta$$ = 90$$^\circ$$, i.e., when the wire is perpendicular to the magnetic field.

Therefore, the correct option is:

Option A: Perpendicular to the magnetic field

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics (Term 1) Paper 2021-22
questions.question: A straight conducting rod of length l and mass m is suspended in a horizontal plane by a pair of flexible strings in a magnetic field of magnitude B. To remove the tension in the supporting strings, the magnitude of the current in the wire is
questions.solution:
To determine the magnitude of the current required in the wire to remove the tension in the supporting strings, we need to analyze the forces acting on the rod in a magnetic field.

The rod experiences a gravitational force $ F_g $ due to its mass. The gravitational force can be written as:

$$ F_g = mg $$

where $ m $ is the mass of the rod and $ g $ is the acceleration due to gravity.

To counteract this gravitational force and remove the tension in the strings, a magnetic force $ F_B $ must act on the rod. The rod experiences a magnetic force when a current flows through it in the presence of a magnetic field. The magnetic force $ F_B $ on a current-carrying conductor in a magnetic field is given by:

$$ F_B = I l B $$

where $ I $ is the current flowing through the rod, $ l $ is the length of the rod, and $ B $ is the magnetic field strength.

To remove the tension in the strings, the magnetic force $ F_B $ must be equal to the gravitational force $ F_g $. Therefore, we have:

$$ mg = I l B $$

Solving for $ I $ (the current required), we get:

$$ I = \frac{mg}{lB} $$

Hence, the correct option is:

Option C

$$ I = \frac{mg}{lB} $$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics (Term 1) Paper 2021-22
questions.question: A constant current is flowing through a solenoid. An iron rod is inserted in the solenoid along its axis. Which of the following quantities will not increase?
options: The magnetic field at the centre
The magnetic flux linked with the solenoid
questions.solution:
When an iron rod is inserted into a solenoid while a constant current is flowing through it, several quantities related to the solenoid and the magnetic field change. Let's analyze each option:

Option A: The magnetic field at the centre

Inserting an iron rod into the solenoid increases the magnetic permeability inside the solenoid. This enhances the magnetic field inside the solenoid because the magnetic field strength $ B $ in a solenoid with a core is given by:

$$ B = \mu \cdot (n \cdot I) $$

where $ \mu $ is the permeability of the core material (greater for iron), $ n $ is the number of turns per unit length, and $ I $ is the current. Thus, the magnetic field at the centre will increase.

Option B: The magnetic flux linked with the solenoid

The magnetic flux $ \Phi $ is given by:

$$ \Phi = B \cdot A $$

where $ B $ is the magnetic field and $ A $ is the cross-sectional area of the solenoid. Since $ B $ increases with the insertion of the iron rod, the magnetic flux linked with the solenoid also increases.

Option C: The rate of heating

The rate of heating in the solenoid depends on the power dissipated, which is given by:

$$ P = I^2 \cdot R $$

where $ I $ is the current and $ R $ is the resistance of the solenoid. The insertion of the iron rod does not directly affect the resistance $ R $ of the solenoid or the current $ I $, assuming a constant current. Therefore, the rate of heating remains unchanged.

Option D: The self-inductance of the solenoid

The self-inductance $ L $ of a solenoid with a core material is given by:

$$ L = \frac{\mu \cdot N^2 \cdot A}{l} $$

where $ \mu $ is the permeability of the core material, $ N $ is the total number of turns, $ A $ is the cross-sectional area, and $ l $ is the length of the solenoid. With the iron rod increasing the permeability $ \mu $, the self-inductance $ L $ will also increase.

Based on this analysis, the quantity that will not increase is:

Option C: The rate of heating

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics (Term 1) Paper 2021-22
questions.question: A proton and an alpha particle move in circular orbits in a uniform magnetic field. Their speeds are in the ratio of 9 : 4. The ratio of radii of their circular orbits $$\left( {{{{r_p}} \over {{r_{alpha}}}}} \right)$$ is
questions.solution:
To find the ratio of the radii of the circular orbits of a proton and an alpha particle in a uniform magnetic field, we need to consider the relationship between the radius of the orbit, the speed of the particle, the charge of the particle, and the mass of the particle.

The radius of the circular orbit in a magnetic field is given by the formula:

$$ r = \frac{mv}{qB} $$

where $ r $ is the radius, $ m $ is the mass of the particle, $ v $ is the speed of the particle, $ q $ is the charge of the particle, and $ B $ is the magnetic field strength.

For the proton ($ p $) and the alpha particle ($ \alpha $), we have the following expressions:

$$ r_p = \frac{m_p v_p}{q_p B} $$

$$ r_\alpha = \frac{m_\alpha v_\alpha}{q_\alpha B} $$

Given that the ratio of the speeds is $ \frac{v_p}{v_\alpha} = \frac{9}{4} $, we can express $ v_p $ and $ v_\alpha $ as $ v_p = 9k $ and $ v_\alpha = 4k $ where $ k $ is a constant.

The charge of a proton $ q_p $ is $ e $ and the mass of a proton $ m_p $ is $ m_p $. The alpha particle has a charge $ q_\alpha = 2e $ and a mass $ m_\alpha = 4m_p $.

Substituting these values into the radius equations, we get:

$$ r_p = \frac{m_p \cdot 9k}{e \cdot B} $$

$$ r_\alpha = \frac{4m_p \cdot 4k}{2e \cdot B} $$

Simplifying the expression for $ r_\alpha $:

$$ r_\alpha = \frac{16m_p \cdot k}{2e \cdot B} = \frac{8m_p \cdot k}{e \cdot B} $$

To find the ratio of the radii $ \left( \frac{r_p}{r_\alpha} \right) $, we divide $ r_p $ by $ r_\alpha $:

$$ \frac{r_p}{r_\alpha} = \frac{\frac{9m_p \cdot k}{e \cdot B}}{\frac{8m_p \cdot k}{e \cdot B}} = \frac{9}{8} $$

Therefore, the ratio of the radii of their circular orbits is:

$$ \frac{r_p}{r_\alpha} = \frac{9}{8} $$

Hence, the correct option is:

$$ \text{Option D:} \frac{9}{8} $$

questions.title: CBSE 12th Physics (Term 1) Paper 2021-22
questions.question:
Assertion (A) : When radius of a current carrying loop is doubled, its magnetic moment becomes four times.

Reason (R) : The magnetic moment of a current carrying loop is directly proportional to the area of the loop.
options: Both (A) & (R) are true and (R) is correct explanation of (A).
Both (A) & (R) are true and (R) is not correct explanation of (A).
(A) is false and (R) is also false.
questions.solution:
To understand the given assertions and reason, let's examine the magnetic moment of a current carrying loop in detail.

The magnetic moment ($ \mu $) of a current carrying loop is given by the formula:

$$ \mu = I \cdot A $$

where $ I $ is the current and $ A $ is the area of the loop.

For a circular loop, the area $ A $ is:

$$ A = \pi r^2 $$

where $ r $ is the radius of the loop.

Therefore, the magnetic moment of a circular loop can be expressed as:

$$ \mu = I \cdot \pi r^2 $$

Now, let's analyze the assertion and reason:

Assertion (A): When the radius of a current carrying loop is doubled, its magnetic moment becomes four times.

If the radius $ r $ is doubled, the new radius will be $ 2r $. The new area $ A' $ will be:

$$ A' = \pi (2r)^2 = 4 \pi r^2 $$

The new magnetic moment $ \mu' $ is:

$$ \mu' = I \cdot A' = I \cdot 4 \pi r^2 = 4 \cdot (I \cdot \pi r^2) = 4 \mu $$

Therefore, we can see that when the radius is doubled, the magnetic moment becomes four times, so Assertion (A) is true.

Reason (R): The magnetic moment of a current carrying loop is directly proportional to the area of the loop.

From the formula $ \mu = I \cdot A $, it is evident that the magnetic moment is directly proportional to the area of the loop. Hence, Reason (R) is also true.

Moreover, the reason (R) correctly explains why the magnetic moment becomes four times when the radius is doubled because the area increases by a factor of four. Therefore, the correct option is:

Option A: Both (A) & (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of (A).

questions.title: JEE Advanced 2021 Paper 1 Online
questions.question:
A long straight wire carries a current, I = 2 ampere. A semi-circular conducting rod is placed beside it on two conducting parallel rails of negligible resistance. Both the rails are parallel to the wire. The wire, the rod and the rails lie in the same horizontal plane, as shown in the figure. Two ends of the semi-circular rod are at the distances 1 cm and 4 cm from the wire. At time t = 0, the rod starts moving on the rails with a speed v = 3.0 m/s (see the figure).

A resistor R = 1.4 $$\Omega$$ and a capacitor C0 = 5.0$$\mu$$F are connected in series between the rails. At time t = 0, C0 is uncharged. Which of the following statement(s) is(are) correct? [$$\mu$$0 = 4$$\pi$$ $$\times$$ 10$$-$$7 SI units. Take ln 2 = 0.7]
options: Maximum current through R is 1.2 $$\times$$ 10<sup>$$-$$6</sup> ampere
Maximum current through R is 3.8 $$\times$$ 10<sup>$$-$$6</sup> ampere
Maximum charge on capacitor C<sub>0</sub> is 8.4 $$\times$$ 10<sup>$$-$$12</sup> coulomb
Maximum charge on capacitor C<sub>0</sub> is 2.4 $$\times$$ 10<sup>$$-$$12</sup> coulomb
questions.solution:
Let a small element of length $d x$ be chosen at a distance $x$ from the wire. Induced emf de across it is given by

$$ \begin{aligned} d \mathrm{E} & =\mathrm{B} v(d x) \\\\ & =\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I}}{2 \pi x} v(d x) \\\\ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{PQ}} & =\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I} v}{2 \pi} \int_1^4 \frac{d x}{x} \\\\ \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{PQ}} & =\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I} v}{2 \pi} \ln 4=\frac{\mu_0 \mathrm{I} v}{2 \pi} \times 2 \ln 2 \end{aligned} $$

On substituting all the values in above, we get

$$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{PQ}} & =2 \times 10^{-7} \times 2 \times 3 \times 2 \times 0.7 \\\\ & =16.8 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned} $$

At $t=0, \mathrm{C}$ acts as a short. So, maximum current flows through $R$.

$$ \begin{aligned} i_{\max } & =\frac{\mathrm{E}_{\mathrm{PQ}}}{\mathrm{R}}=\frac{16.8 \times 10^{-7}}{1.4} \\\\ & =12 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~A} \\\\ & =1.2 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned} $$

At $t \rightarrow \infty, C$ acts as on open. So,

$$ \begin{aligned} q_{\max } & =\mathrm{CE}_{\mathrm{PQ}}=5 \times 10^{-6} \times 16.8 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{C} \\\\ & =84 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{C} \\\\ & =8.4 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C} \end{aligned} $$

They are accelerated through a potential V and then allowed to pass into a region of uniform magnetic field which is normal to the velocities of the particles. Within this region, the $$\alpha$$-particle and the sulfur ion move in circular orbits of radii r<sub>$$\alpha$$</sub> and r<sub>s</sub>, respectively. The ratio (r<sub>s</sub>/r<sub>$$\alpha$$</sub>) is __________.

$${r_\alpha } = {{\sqrt {2{m_\alpha }{q_\alpha }V} } \over {{q_\alpha }B}}$$

$${{{r_s}} \over {{r_\alpha }}} = \sqrt {{{{m_s}} \over {{q_s}}}{{{q_\alpha }} \over {{m_\alpha }}}} = \sqrt {\left( {{{32} \over 1}} \right)\left( {{2 \over 4}} \right)} $$

$${{{r_s}} \over {{r_\alpha }}} = 4$$

The smaller loop carries current I1 in the anti-clockwise direction and the larger loop carries current I2 in the clockwise direction, with I2 > 2I1. $$\overrightarrow B $$(x, y) denotes the magnetic field at a point (x, y) in the xy-plane. questions.title: BITSAT 2021
questions.question: Which of the following is always true?
questions.solution:
Since, $$\sim$$ (p $$\vee$$ q) $$\equiv$$ ($$\sim$$ p $$\wedge$$ $$\sim$$ q) and $$\sim$$ (p $$\wedge$$ q) $$\equiv$$ ($$\sim$$ p $$\vee$$ $$\sim$$ q)

So, options (a) and (d) are not true.

$$\sim$$ (p $$\to$$ q) $$\equiv$$ p $$\wedge$$ $$\sim$$ q, so option (c) is not true.

Now, p $$\to$$ q $$\sim$$ p $$\vee$$ q

$$\sim$$ q $$\to$$ $$\sim$$ p $$\equiv$$ [$$\sim$$ ($$\sim$$ q) $$\vee$$ $$\sim$$ p]

$$\equiv$$ q $$\vee$$ $$\sim$$ p $$\equiv$$ p $$\vee$$ q $$\Rightarrow$$ p $$\to$$ q $$\equiv$$ ($$\sim$$ q $$\to$$ $$\sim$$ p)

questions.title: BITSAT 2021
questions.solution:
Given, $$\left| {\matrix{ 0 & {x - p} & {x - q} \cr {x + p} & 0 & {x - r} \cr {x + q} & {x - r} & 0 \cr } } \right| = 0$$

$$ - (x - p)\left| {\matrix{ {x + p} & {x - r} \cr {x + q} & 0 \cr } } \right| + (x - q)\left| {\matrix{ {x + p} & 0 \cr {x + q} & {x - r} \cr } } \right| = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (x - p)(x + q)(x - r) + (x - q)(x + p)(x - r) = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (x - r)[(x - p)(x + q) + (x - q)(x + p)] = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (x - r)[{x^2} - px + qx - pq + {x^2} - qx + px - pq] = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow (x - r)[2{x^2} - 2pq] = 0 \Rightarrow x - r = 0$$ or $${x^2} - pq = 0$$

either x = r or pq
